key_criteria: Dark Ah ≥10 cm; base saturation ≥80%; no Btj/Bnj; not gleyed; Ah color value ≥3.5 moist or chroma ≥2 dry in upper 10 cm
notes: Driest zone; annual precip typically <350 mm; Brown Ah reflects lower organic matter input

key_criteria: Dark Ah ≥10 cm; base saturation ≥80%; no Btj/Bnj; Ah darker than Brown Chernozem; intermediate moisture zone
notes: Transitional between Brown and Black zones; precip ~350–450 mm

key_criteria: Very dark Ah ≥10 cm (value ≤2 moist, chroma ≤2); base saturation ≥80%; highest organic carbon of Chernozemic order; no Btj/Bnj
notes: Most productive agricultural soils; precip ~450–550 mm; common in central Alberta parkland

key_horizons: Ah, Ae or AB, Bt or Bm
key_criteria: Dark Ah but with eluviation features (Ae or AB); transitional to Luvisolic; base saturation ≥80%; no Btj; not gleyed
notes: Forest-grassland transition; grey tone in Ah indicates leaching; common in aspen parkland

key_criteria: Bnt (natric B) with columnar or prismatic structure; high exchangeable Na (ESP ≥15%); abrupt textural change at top of Bnt; may have thin Ae
notes: Associated with saline-sodic depressions and pothole wetlands; restricts drainage

key_criteria: Ae horizon ≥2 cm over Bnt; Bnt has columnar/prismatic structure; more leached than Solonetz but natric B intact
notes: Wetted depressions with seasonal ponding; intermediate degradation stage; common around saline lakes

key_criteria: Strongly degraded Bnt — columnar structure largely destroyed; thick Ae; low remaining Na; near-complete leaching of natric horizon
notes: Advanced degradation of Solonetz under prolonged leaching; structurally similar to Gray Luvisol

key_criteria: Ae over Bt (argillic B with clay films); base saturation ≥50%; warm temperate climate; Great Lakes/St. Lawrence region
notes: Not found in Alberta; restricted to Great Lakes–St. Lawrence lowlands

key_criteria: Ae over Bt (argillic B with clay skins/films); base saturation ≥50%; boreal/sub-boreal climate; no permafrost within 2 m; no prolonged saturation
notes: Dominant upland forest soil in boreal Alberta; Ae-Bt sequence is diagnostic; common parent for Luvic Gleysols

key_criteria: Bh horizon (organic matter illuviation with little Fe/Al); carbon/pyrophosphate-Al ratio >5; typically under heath or wet forest; may be seasonally saturated
notes: Associated with acidic wet depressions and sphagnum-dominated areas; bh horizon accumulates organic carbon

key_criteria: Bhf horizon with both organic matter and Fe-Al accumulation; pyrophosphate-Al ≥0.5%; C/pyrophosphate-Al ratio 0.5–5; typically well-drained
notes: High-rainfall or coarse-textured parent materials; less common in Alberta than in coastal or shield areas

key_criteria: Bf horizon dominant — Al and Fe with little organic matter; C/pyrophosphate-Al ratio <0.5; typically well-drained coarse soils under coniferous forest
notes: Most common Podzol in Alberta; coarse-textured glaciofluvial or aeolian parent materials

key_criteria: Dark Ah (≥10 cm, value ≤3 moist); Bm (altered, no clay films, no podzolic B); base saturation of Bm ≥80%; not gleyed
notes: Dark Ah separates from Eutric; well-drained; some overlap with Chernozemic in transition zones

key_criteria: Bm present (color or structure alteration); base saturation ≥50%; Ah <10 cm or absent; no podzolic B; no prolonged saturation
notes: Common on calcareous or base-rich parent materials; broad category covering many moderately developed soils

key_criteria: Bh horizon but pyrophosphate-Al <0.5% (distinguishes from Podzol); forested; Ah often present; base saturation variable
notes: Restricted to specific parent material/climate combinations; uncommon in Alberta

key_criteria: Bm present; base saturation <50% throughout; no podzolic B; acidic parent materials (siliceous tills, sands); not gleyed
notes: Acidic sandy or siliceous substrates; leached but without podzolization; common on coarse shield-derived materials

key_criteria: No diagnostic B horizon; no thick Ah (≥10 cm); surface is C or Ck or similar; recently deposited, eroded, or very sandy parent material
notes: Floodplains, active alluvial fans, dunes, recent cutbanks; frequently disturbed or youthful soils

key_criteria: Ah ≥10 cm (dark, organic-rich) but no diagnostic B; parent material too coarse/young for B development despite organic accumulation
notes: Meadows on coarse outwash or recent fluvial deposits; transitional environments where organics accumulate without pedogenesis

key_horizons: Ae or Ahe, Btg or Btjg, Cg
key_criteria: Gleyed argillic B (Btg or Btjg) with clay films; gleying within 50 cm; Ae present; mottles and reduced matrix indicative of seasonal saturation
notes: Mineral wetlands on fine-textured glacial till with clay illuviation; seasonally flooded depressions; common in parkland Alberta

key_horizons: Ah or Ahe (≥10 cm), Bg or Cg
key_criteria: Ah ≥10 cm (dark, high organic C) overlying gleyed B or C; gleying within 50 cm of surface; no argillic B; saturated for significant part of growing season
notes: Sedge fens, wet meadows, marshes on mineral substrates; thick Ah distinguishes from Gleysol; highly relevant for wetland classification

key_criteria: Gleying within 50 cm of surface; Ah <10 cm; no argillic B; dominated by mottles, gray/blue-gray matrix, or reduced conditions; seasonally to permanently saturated
notes: Open water margins, poorly drained flats, shallow mineral wetlands; most common mineral wetland soil in Alberta

key_criteria: Organic material ≥40 cm; dominant layers are fibric (H1–H4 Von Post; >40% fibre after rubbing); mostly undecomposed plant remains visible; low bulk density
notes: Bogs and poor fens with sphagnum; relatively undecomposed peat; lowest degree of humification; typical of raised bogs and some flat fens

key_criteria: Organic material ≥40 cm; dominant layers are mesic (H5–H7 Von Post; 17–40% fibre after rubbing); intermediate decomposition; some original plant structure visible
notes: Rich and moderate fens; partially decomposed peat; most common peat type in Alberta; transitional between fibric and humic

key_criteria: Organic material ≥40 cm; dominant layers are humic (H8–H10 Von Post; <17% fibre after rubbing); highly decomposed; original plant structure largely destroyed; high bulk density
notes: Highly decomposed fen or bog peat; often at depth; lower water table or longer decomposition history; associated with drained or older peatlands

key_criteria: ≥40 cm of organic material but aerobic decomposition (forest litter, mor humus over rock or coarse fragments); not water-saturated; over bedrock, boulders, or coarse rubble
notes: Forest floor accumulation on rocky or coarse substrates; NOT a wetland soil despite organic classification; differentiated by aerobic conditions

key_criteria: Permafrost within 1 m of surface; evidence of cryoturbation (disrupted, involuted, or broken horizons); patterned ground features at surface; active layer seasonal thaw
notes: Permafrost peatlands and mineral soils in northern Alberta; cryoturbation mixes organic and mineral material; common in patterned fens

key_criteria: Permafrost within 2 m of surface; no cryoturbation features; horizons intact; permafrost table may be deeper than Turbic
notes: Less disturbed permafrost soils; northern boreal and subarctic Alberta; may support peatland or mineral wetland vegetation

key_horizons: Of/Om/Oh, permafrost within 1 m below organic
key_criteria: ≥40 cm organic material over permafrost within 1 m of mineral surface; combines Organic and Cryosolic criteria; peatland with near-surface permafrost
notes: Permafrost peatlands (palsas, peat plateaus) in northern Alberta; highly sensitive to climate change; permafrost thaw converts to Mesisol or Humisol

key_criteria: Ah ≥10 cm over vertic B or C; >30% smectite clay throughout; deep (>50 cm) cracks when dry; slickensides (Bss); gilgai microrelief may be present
notes: Clay-rich lacustrine deposits in Alberta interior; cracks seasonally; Ah distinguishes from Vertisol; limited to specific lake-bed parent materials

key_criteria: No significant Ah (<10 cm); >30% smectite clay; deep seasonal cracking; slickensides; gilgai microrelief; swells when wet, shrinks when dry
notes: Exposed lacustrine clays; very limited distribution in Alberta; parent material control dominant; little pedogenic differentiation
